chunk_summary: This section discusses monitoring linear growth in children and adolescents, focusing on early indicators, anthropometric response timelines, and criteria for escalation in non-responders to nutritional interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of monitoring short-interval process indicators for linear growth response.
Learn how to assess growth velocity and interpret anthropometric data over 8–12 weeks.
Identify criteria for defining non-response and when to escalate evaluation or refer cases.
Recognize red flags such as disproportion, pubertal arrest, or systemic symptoms in growth monitoring.
key_facts: Short-interval process indicators include meal structure, protein intake, IFA supplementation, deworming, and morbidity reduction (tracked over 2–4 weeks).
Height trajectory changes require 8–12 weeks; typical mid-childhood growth velocity is 5–6 cm/year.
Non-response is defined as no improvement in growth velocity or continued downward height Z score after 8–12 weeks of adherence and reduced morbidity.
Red flags for escalation include height < -3 SD, pubertal arrest, chronic diarrhea, organomegaly, or persistent systemic symptoms.
Delayed puberty can mimic growth failure in adolescents, requiring careful interpretation of growth velocity.
reference_quote: In mid-childhood, a typical reference range is about 5–6 cm/year; persistent velocity clearly below this, especially with continued centile crossing, should not be labeled 'just stunting.'
document_key_topics: Monitoring linear growth response in children and adolescents
Defining non-response and criteria for escalation
Anthropometric assessment and growth velocity interpretation
Red flags and referral guidelines in growth monitoring
